focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

By Monday morning Nate had a legal pad full of arrows, three photocopied site plans, and a headache from trying to learn commercial redevelopment the way he had once learned why a dentist's office could print from one machine but not the other: not by understanding the whole system, exactly, but by tracing every dependency until the thing coughed up a shape.

He had spent the week after Mort's store in Dominic's sent mail, in zoning packets, in rent rolls, in the Eastbridge file, in photocopies that came out of the office machine warm and smelling faintly of burnt dust. He had found Dominic's old arguments in pieces. An email from March about relocation assistance. A margin note on a site plan in Dominic's handwriting, a quick slanted block of capitals next to the end unit: PRESERVE PHASE 1? A yellow sticky on a demolition schedule with the adhesive gone dry enough that it came off in Nate's hand. Somebody had written "holiday optics" on it and underlined it twice. Dominic or Ray or Victor or some lawyer Nate would never meet. It did not matter. The whole office was full of fingerprints that were not fingerprints, traces of people making choices and then leaving the paper behind.

His revised proposal sat in a manila folder on Dominic's desk with a title page he had printed twice because the first one skewed in the copier: EASTBRIDGE RETAIL TRANSITION PLAN. The title sounded like bullshit, which was probably how he knew it belonged in this building. Underneath the title page was a cover memo, then the marked-up footprint, then a page of rough numbers showing what happened if they preserved the end units through phase one, kept Lazar's Hardware operating, maybe kept Gail's if the common wall could be braced, and folded both businesses into the finished reta...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...k of a finger, then wiped that finger on his sweatpants. The gesture was stupid and automatic and did nothing.

It had been since July, since her hand had gone behind his neck and trusted what it found there. Fraud was too clean a word for a bed.

From the living room came the soft rustle of paper, then the tiny click of her glasses. Sarah wasn't asleep. He pictured her on the couch under the spare blanket, knees bent because the couch wasn't long enough, the cushion seam grinding into her hip.

He sat up.

For a second he thought he might actually do it. He put his feet on the floor, crossed the bedroom, and opened the door.

The lamp was still on in the living room. Sarah was a shape under the blanket, turned away, one shoulder making a small ridge. Her tote bag sat on the floor beside the couch, one paper halfway out like a tongue.

He stepped into the hall. The floorboard near the bathroom creaked.

"What?" she said.

Her voice was tired, not inviting, not curious.

He put his hand on the wall. The paint felt cold. He had come out to tell the truth, but his tongue had gone thick and useless.

"Sarah," he said.

She waited.

The months since July hit him in pieces instead of order: her thumb under his jaw in the kitchen, her calf sliding over his in the dark, Dominic's name pushed into the pillow because that was the man she thought she had.

"I'm sorry," he said.

She let out a breath that wasn't quite a laugh. "That isn't enough."

No. It wasn't.

He stood there until the wall warmed under his palm. Then he went back into the bedroom, got under the covers, and stayed on his own half of the mattress like that counted as anything.

Out in the living room the couch springs gave once as she shifted. He pictured the seam digging into her hip again and still didn't move.
